title: Writing a SKILL.md That Triggers
objective: Author the frontmatter and body well: field constraints, naming, trigger-worthy descriptions, conciseness, and degrees of freedom.

sources.title: Agent Skills — Overview (Claude Platform Docs)
sources.trust_rationale: Anthropic's canonical product documentation for Agent Skills.

sources.title: Skill authoring best practices (Claude Platform Docs)

beats.narration: Let's write one properly, starting with the frontmatter rules. The name is capped at sixty-four characters, allows only lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens, and — a common gotcha — it cannot contain the reserved words anthropic or claude. The description is capped at one thousand twenty-four characters, must be non-empty, and, like the name, cannot contain XML tags.

visual.alt: The name and description fields with their length and character constraints

beats.narration: For the name itself, Anthropic recommends the gerund form — verb plus i-n-g — because it describes the activity: processing-pdfs, analyzing-spreadsheets, managing-databases. Noun phrases like pdf-processing are fine too. What you want to avoid are vague names like helper, utils, or tools, and generic ones like documents or data. A sharp, consistent name pays off across a whole library of skills.

visual.alt: Good gerund names versus vague names to avoid

beats.narration: Now the single most important line you'll write: the description. Claude uses it to choose the right skill from potentially a hundred or more. So make it specific, include the key terms a user would say, and state both what the skill does and when to use it. And always write it in third person — it gets injected into the system prompt, so first or second person phrasing causes discovery problems.

visual.alt: A good specific third-person description versus vague first-person ones

beats.narration_intro: Pick the description that will trigger reliably.
beats.prompt: Which description is most likely to help Claude select the skill correctly?

options.text: Analyzes Excel files; use for .xlsx or tabular data
I can help you out with all kinds of data tasks
Processes assorted data of whatever type you need

beats.correct_feedback: Correct. It's third person, specific, and states both what it does and when to use it.
beats.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. The strongest description is specific and third person, naming both what the skill does and when to use it — the Excel option.

beats.narration: For the body, the governing principle is: concise is key. Your default assumption should be that Claude is already very smart, so only add context Claude doesn't already have. Don't explain what a PDF is or how libraries work. Challenge every paragraph with one question — does this justify its token cost? Once loaded, each line competes with conversation history and everything else.

visual.alt: A verbose 150-token instruction versus a concise 50-token one

beats.narration: The other big authoring decision is how much freedom to give. Match specificity to the task's fragility. High freedom — plain prose — when many approaches are valid and context decides. Medium freedom — a parameterised pattern — when there's a preferred way but some variation is fine. Low freedom — an exact script you tell Claude not to modify — when the operation is fragile and consistency is critical. Narrow bridge: guardrails. Open field: direction.

visual.alt: High, medium, and low freedom instruction styles with the bridge and field analogy

beats.narration_intro: Match the task to the freedom level.
beats.prompt: A fragile database migration that must run in an exact sequence calls for which style?

options.text: Low freedom: a specific script Claude runs exactly as given
High freedom: open prose leaving Claude to choose freely
No instructions at all, so Claude stays flexible here

beats.correct_feedback: Right. Fragile, must-be-consistent operations get low freedom — an exact script, not modified.
beats.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. A fragile migration that must run in an exact sequence calls for low freedom — a specific script Claude runs without modifying.

beats.narration: Two habits keep a skill healthy over time. First, use consistent terminology — pick one word, always API endpoint, always field, always extract, rather than rotating synonyms Claude has to reconcile. Second, avoid time-sensitive information. Don't write before August use the old API. Instead keep a Current method section and tuck deprecated details into an Old patterns section, so the skill never silently goes wrong.

visual.alt: Consistent terminology and an old-patterns section for deprecated details
